data.title: Occidental targets 1.45M boe/d production in 2026 with $5.5B-$5.9B capital plan as cost efficiencies deepen
data.description:
Earnings Call Insights: Occidental Petroleum Corporation (OXY) Q4 2025

Management View

  • Vicki Hollub, President and CEO, described 2025 as an exceptional year, emphasizing that "the sale of OxyChem made possible by the quality of our portfolio was a deliberate step to strengthen our balance sheet and enable us to deliver greater value from our high-return oil and gas assets." Hollub noted a new annual production record of 1.4 million barrels of oil equivalent per day, surpassing guidance while spending $300 million less in oil and gas capital than planned and reducing annual operating expenses by $275 million. She highlighted the completion of a 10-year portfolio transformation, stating, "we no longer require transformative acquisitions. Instead, our teams are focused on what they do best, and that is execution, including cost reduction, capital efficiency and well performance."
  • Sunil Mathew, CFO, stated, "In the fourth quarter, we delivered strong operational and financial results. We generated an adjusted profit of $0.31 per diluted share and a reported loss of ($0.07) per diluted share. The difference was largely driven by charges and transaction costs related to the sale of OxyChem." He highlighted free cash flow generation of approximately $1 billion despite lower realized oil prices and emphasized the successful reduction of principal debt to $15 billion, with a $700 million tender offer announced to further reduce debt to $14.3 billion.
  • Richard Jackson, COO, emphasized operational achievements, including record production, a 15% reduction in new well capital costs in U.S. onshore, and ongoing cost efficiency efforts that delivered approximately $2 billion in annual oil and gas cost savings since 2023. He noted that "these efficiencies further enabled us to reduce U.S. onshore capital by $400 million compared to 2025 while still delivering a 1% production growth."
  • Hollub announced that Jordan Tanner will transition to a leadership role in the Gulf of America, with Babatunde Cole appointed as Vice President of Investor Relations.

Outlook

  • Hollub outlined 2026 priorities: maintaining production base through safe operations, sustaining and growing dividends (including an 8% increase to the quarterly dividend), and further strengthening the financial position with continued net debt reductions. She said, "We expect capital spending to range from $5.5 billion to $5.9 billion, representing a $550 million reduction from 2025, excluding OxyChem."
  • Mathew projected, "we expect production to average approximately 1.45 million barrels of oil equivalent per day," and said, "we expect to improve free cash flow by more than $1.2 billion in 2026."
  • The company expects approximately 70% of oil and gas capital to be allocated to U.S. onshore operations, with flexibility to respond to commodity price improvements.

Financial Results

  • Occidental generated $4.3 billion in free cash flow before working capital in 2025, even as oil prices declined by around 14% from 2024.
  • On a normalized basis and excluding OxyChem, cash flow from operations increased by 27% year-over-year.
  • Principal debt reduced to $15 billion, with a tender offer expected to bring this to $14.3 billion.
  • The Midstream segment exceeded the midpoint of guidance by more than $500 million in adjusted pretax income for the year; Q4 adjusted pretax income exceeded guidance by $172 million.
  • The company achieved its lowest lease operating expense per barrel of oil equivalent since 2021, with Q4 domestic operating expense at $7.77 per BOE.

Q&A

  • Arun Jayaram, JPMorgan: Asked about the lower CapEx guide and the drivers of the $800 million reduction. Hollub stated, "our teams came up with these ideas on what we should do and what were the best projects. But as they continue to optimize those projects, it was pretty amazing to see the cost that they were able to cut out, the efficiencies that they were able to find."
  • Jayaram also asked about the Horn Mountain waterflood project and its potential to support a sustaining production profile in the Gulf. Dillon responded, "this waterflood, the King dump flood, the future waterfloods... will move from a 20% to sub-10% decline by 2030 and improving to below 5% in subsequent years."
  • Nitin Kumar, Mizuho: Inquired about the unconventional portion of the sub-$30 per barrel breakeven resource. Hollub explained, "the secondary benches are providing now as much value as the primary benches did... the average resource is about a $38 per barrel breakeven."
  • Kumar followed up on share buybacks. Mathew said, "we would like to first get our principal debt to $10 billion, but we are not setting a time frame to get to this target as we want to have some flexibility."
  • Wei Jiang, Barclays: Explored the sustainability of cost savings into 2027. Mathew noted, "we expect to maintain this momentum into next year. So we could see a modest growth with this year's capital depending on the efficiency and new well performance."
  • Douglas Leggate, Wolfe Research: Asked about sustaining capital at different oil prices and the outlook for LCV. Mathew outlined how sustaining capital is calculated, "from $5.7 billion, you back out LCV and exploration of $300 million, you're at $5.4 billion... and going from $5.2 billion to $4.1 billion at $40, that is primarily deflation, around 20% deflation."

Sentiment Analysis

  • Analysts focused on CapEx reductions, cost efficiency sustainability, capital allocation flexibility, and sustaining production levels, with a generally positive but probing tone. Multiple questions centered on structural cost savings and their impact on future years.
  • Management maintained a confident tone in prepared remarks, using phrases like "we are perfectly positioned" and "I'm confident that our teams will continue to innovate." In Q&A, management continued to emphasize operational excellence and cost discipline, reiterating flexibility and the sustainability of improvements.
  • Compared to the previous quarter, management's tone remained confident but placed greater emphasis on cost discipline and flexibility, while analysts maintained a constructive but slightly more detailed and probing approach regarding the durability of efficiencies.

Quarter-over-Quarter Comparison

  • Guidance for 2026 capital spending was notably reduced to $5.5 billion–$5.9 billion from the previous "soft guide" range of $6.3 billion–$6.7 billion, reflecting deeper cost efficiencies and project optimization.
  • Dividend growth focus strengthened with the announcement of an 8% increase.
  • Debt reduction accelerated, with the principal debt moving to $15 billion and a new target of $14.3 billion.
  • Analysts in both quarters questioned the sustainability of cost reductions and the impact of capital allocation, but current quarter questions showed greater scrutiny on the structural nature of savings and the long-term outlook for sustaining capital.
  • Management confidence increased regarding the resource base and operational agility, highlighting a shift from transformative acquisitions to execution and efficiency as the core strategy.

Risks and Concerns

  • Management noted the ongoing industry challenge of reserves replacement and the importance of maintaining a high reserves replacement ratio.
  • The outlook for oil prices remains uncertain, with Hollub expressing caution about the sustainability of recent geopolitical price spikes.
  • Analysts raised questions about the durability of cost savings, the ability to sustain production growth, and the flexibility of capital allocation in different macro scenarios.
  • Mathew acknowledged that no timeframe is set for reaching the $10 billion principal debt target, signaling market-dependent flexibility.

Final Takeaway

Occidental Petroleum delivered a year of record operational and financial performance, marked by substantial cost efficiencies, balance sheet strengthening, and a strategic focus on sustaining and growing shareholder returns. The company’s 2026 plan projects lower capital spending of $5.5 billion to $5.9 billion while targeting average production of 1.45 million BOE per day. Management emphasized continued cost discipline, resilient free cash flow growth, and capital allocation flexibility, positioning Oxy for long-term value creation amid uncertain commodity markets and industry-wide challenges in reserves replacement.

Read the full Earnings Call Transcript

data.title: Upbound Group outlines $4.7B–$4.95B 2026 revenue target while advancing digital growth and Brigit integration
data.description:
Earnings Call Insights: Upbound Group, Inc. (UPBD) Q4 2025

Management View

  • Fahmi Karam, CEO, highlighted 2025 as a year of “significant progress” for Upbound, noting the company served over 3.5 million customers and expanded its business through the acquisition of Brigit. He emphasized Upbound’s transformation into a digital and data-driven platform for underserved consumers, stating, “Our team's dedication and shared vision have driven key achievements that we believe strongly position Upbound for continued success and long-term growth.”
  • The addition of Brigit, a subscription-based financial health technology company, was described as a milestone, with Karam underscoring its “remarkable growth and delivered significant value to its users.” He cited the pilot of a new line of credit offering and cross-selling initiatives as key developments.
  • Two executive appointments were announced: Hal Khouri as CFO and Rebecca Wooters as Chief Growth Officer. Karam described Wooters’ role as leading “digital transformation and initiatives and implement data-driven solutions across all 3 major segments of the company.”
  • Karam reported that revenue grew 8.7% to approximately $4.7 billion, and non-GAAP diluted EPS reached $4.13, near the high end of guidance. He added, “Our cash flow generation was particularly strong in 2025 with free cash flow of $180 million, increasing over $130 million year-over-year.”
  • Hal Khouri, CFO, stated, “Acima revenue grew 8.6% year-over-year, which was its ninth consecutive quarter of revenue growth and adjusted EBITDA of $87 million was up 7.3% from a year ago.” He also noted, “Brigit finished the quarter with approximately 1.6 million paid subscribers, which was a nearly 30% increase from the year ago period.”

Outlook

  • Upbound projects 2026 consolidated revenue in the range of $4.7 billion to $4.95 billion, adjusted EBITDA of $500 million to $535 million, and fully diluted non-GAAP EPS of $4 to $4.35. Free cash flow is expected to increase to approximately $200 million.
  • Acima’s 2026 GMV and revenue are expected to increase mid-single digits year-over-year, with adjusted EBITDA margin in line with 2025 and losses “stabilizing in the 9.5% area for the year.”
  • Brigit is expected to deliver annualized revenue growth of over 30% in the $265 million to $285 million range and adjusted EBITDA in the $50 million to $60 million range. Management cited delays in new product rollouts and macro uncertainty as impacting the variance from initial estimates.
  • Rent-A-Center’s 2026 revenue is expected to be flat to positive relative to 2025, with adjusted EBITDA margins remaining stable.

Financial Results

  • 2025 revenue reached approximately $4.7 billion, the highest in company history. Adjusted EBITDA was nearly $510 million.
  • Fourth quarter consolidated revenue was $1.2 billion, a 10.9% increase year-over-year, with the Brigit segment and Acima’s 8.6% growth as primary drivers.
  • Non-GAAP diluted EPS for Q4 was $1.01. Free cash flow for the year was $180 million, and liquidity at year-end stood at $358 million.
  • Acima’s fourth quarter GMV was nearly $550 million, with a loss rate of 10.1% in line with prior guidance. Brigit recorded $64.6 million of Q4 revenue and a 17.2% adjusted EBITDA margin.
  • Rent-A-Center delivered nearly $480 million in Q4 revenue, with same-store sales growth of 80 basis points and an adjusted EBITDA margin of 14.4%.

Q&A

  • Robert Griffin, Raymond James, asked about the seasonality in EPS guidance. CEO Karam explained, “you have a full benefit of a full quarter of Brigit being in the numbers. So... you only had 2/3 of the quarter with Brigit on our books.”
  • Griffin inquired about Brigit’s guidance and integration. Karam responded that delays in new product launches and macro uncertainty were key factors, adding, “We want to be very cautious... around our loss profile and kind of judging where the consumer is.” He described integration as “light touch” so far, with more substantial integration planned for late 2026 into 2027.
  • Vincent Caintic, BTIG, asked about Acima’s GMV cadence. Karam stated, “the first half of the year will be relatively flat... and then we kind of get back to a seamless norm as we lap some of the changes that we made in the second half of the year.”
  • Kyle Joseph, Stephens, asked about tax refund impacts. Karam indicated a normal tax season was assumed, with higher refunds potentially benefiting revenue but possibly lowering gross profit margin.
  • Hoang Nguyen, TD Cowen, inquired about Brigit competition and revenue cadence. Karam said the competitive landscape was intensifying but emphasized differentiation through product and feature enhancements, expecting subscriber growth to be “back-end loaded.”
  • Yuna Sohn, Jefferies, asked about margin trends. CFO Khouri explained, “the competitive landscape... is putting pressure across the board on margins,” with higher costs and tariffs impacting profitability.

Sentiment Analysis

  • Analysts’ tone was probing and focused on clarifying guidance shape, product integration, and competitive pressures, with repeated questions on Brigit’s outlook and Acima’s credit tightening.
  • Management maintained a confident and measured tone in prepared remarks, shifting to a more cautious stance in Q&A, especially regarding Brigit’s delayed rollout and macro uncertainties. Phrases such as “We want to be very cautious” and “a combination of things that has caused us to slow down” indicated measured optimism.
  • Compared to the previous quarter, management’s sentiment shifted from emphasizing innovation and growth to a more balanced message of opportunity and risk management. Analysts’ tone remained consistently diligent, pressing for specifics on growth drivers and risk factors.

Quarter-over-Quarter Comparison

  • Guidance for 2026 consolidated revenue and non-GAAP EPS is largely consistent with previous projections but reflects more cautious assumptions for Brigit’s growth, noting “delays in new products” and “macro uncertainty.”
  • The previous quarter highlighted the onboarding of new executives and strong Brigit momentum, while the current quarter focuses on execution and integration challenges.
  • Analysts in both quarters emphasized clarity on underwriting tightening and the impact on GMV and losses, but current questions intensified around Brigit’s product delays and the competitive environment.
  • Management’s tone evolved from highlighting aggressive innovation to expressing disciplined growth and integration pacing.

Risks and Concerns

  • Management noted that the “core consumer continues to navigate a challenging environment, including the cumulative effects of inflation and elevated prices for essentials like groceries, rent and utilities, which weigh on their purchasing power.”
  • Both Acima and Rent-A-Center implemented “targeted risk management adjustments” in response to evolving macro conditions.
  • Brigit’s outlook was tempered by “delays in new product rollouts” and “macro uncertainty,” leading to conservative guidance.
  • Legal and regulatory matters remain, with $72 million accrued for settlements, primarily relating to two ongoing cases.
  • Analysts raised concerns over margin pressure, competitive threats in Brigit’s space, and the sustainability of recent improvements in loss rates and GMV growth.

Final Takeaway

Upbound Group’s 2025 performance reflected strong execution on digital transformation and portfolio diversification, with the Brigit acquisition and executive team expansion playing central roles. Looking ahead, the company projects continued growth and stable profitability in 2026, but with a more measured approach to product launches and integration, particularly at Brigit. Management remains focused on operational excellence, disciplined credit risk, and leveraging data-driven insights to drive cross-segment synergies while navigating ongoing macro pressures and regulatory challenges.

Read the full Earnings Call Transcript

data.title: Candel Therapeutics launches $100M public stock offering; shares down
data.description:
• Candel Therapeutics (CADL) announced an underwritten public offering of $100M of its common stock.
  • CADL plans to grant underwriters a 30-day option to purchase up to an additional $15M in shares.
  • All shares in the offering will be issued and sold by Candel.
  • Net proceeds will fund launch readiness, medical affairs, and commercialization efforts for aglatimagene besadenovec (CAN-2409) in early, localized prostate cancer.
  • Funds will also support the ongoing Phase 3 trial of aglatimagene in non-small cell lung cancer and general corporate purposes.
  • CADL shares down 10.8% post-market.

data.title: Manulife launches share buyback of up to 42 million shares
data.description:
• Manulife Financial Corporation (MFC) on Thursday said that it has received approval from the Toronto Stock Exchange to repurchase for cancellation up to 42 million common shares under a normal course issuer bid.
  • The buyback represents about 2.5% of the company’s issued and outstanding common shares as of February 10, 2026.
  • Purchases may begin on February 24, 2026 and continue until February 23, 2027, or earlier if completed.
  • Manulife said it may purchase shares on the Toronto Stock Exchange, the New York Stock Exchange and alternative trading systems in Canada and the United States at prevailing market prices.
  • All shares acquired under the program will be cancelled.
  • The company has also entered into an automatic share purchase plan with a designated broker to facilitate purchases under the program.
  • MFC -0.0% after hours to $36.17.
  • Source: Press Release

data.title: Hamilton Insurance Group reports Q4 results
data.description:
• Hamilton Insurance Group press release (HG): Q4 net income of $172.2 million, or $1.69 per diluted share, and operating income of $168.2 million, or $1.65 per diluted share.
  • Gross premiums written of $669.0 million, an increase of 23.0% compared to the fourth quarter of 2024.
  • Net premiums earned of $576.7 million, an increase of 19.7% compared to the fourth quarter of 2024.

data.title: Bandwidth outlines 16% revenue growth target for 2026 amid accelerating enterprise AI adoption and $80M share repurchase program
data.description:
Earnings Call Insights: Bandwidth Inc. (BAND) Q4 2025

Management View

  • CEO David Morken reported a solid fourth quarter, emphasizing "sustained business performance and strengthening fundamentals." He highlighted record $1 million-plus enterprise deals in Q4, strong progress with high-margin innovation, and tangible results from AI voice tools, the Trust portfolio, the Global Communications Cloud, and Maestro orchestration software.
  • Morken stated, "When we reported 2022 results, we set ambitious 4-year goals extending through 2026, a 15% to 20% revenue compound annual growth rate, a 5 percentage point increase in gross margin to 60%, a 20% EBITDA margin, a 15% free cash flow margin and $125 million in cumulative free cash flow." He acknowledged that messaging market dynamics will likely prevent achieving the revenue CAGR target but confirmed Bandwidth is "fully on track" for margin and free cash flow objectives, having already surpassed the $125 million cumulative free cash flow goal.
  • Bandwidth announced its first-ever share repurchase program, reflecting confidence in its "durability of our business model and our ability to generate cash while simultaneously investing in our future."
  • Morken described AI-driven enterprise wins, including a top U.S. insurance group and a top 10 U.S. bank, as well as momentum in RCS messaging and a quadrupling of third-party conversational AI developers on the platform in the last six months.
  • CFO Daryl Raiford reported, "In the fourth quarter of 2025, strong execution drove solid revenue and record levels of profitability and free cash flow. Total revenue saw a 12% year-over-year increase on an organic basis." Both Voice and Messaging segments delivered double-digit growth, with voice up 11% and messaging up 12% year-over-year.
  • Raiford noted, "EBITDA margin reached 17%, reflecting improved pricing and mix and continued progress on profitability."

Outlook

  • Raiford outlined 2026 full year guidance: "total revenue growth of approximately 16% year-over-year, including cloud communications growth of approximately 10%, adjusted EBITDA improvement of nearly 30% year-over-year, in line with our aim to achieve a 20% full year adjusted EBITDA margin and non-GAAP earnings per share of approximately $1.66 to $1.74, representing growth of approximately 19%."
  • The company expects "continued accelerating revenue growth in voice," driven by AI-influenced usage, large deals, software services, and geographic expansion.
  • Political campaign messaging is projected to contribute "roughly 2.5% of cloud communications revenue" in 2026.
  • Management confirmed that 2026 outlook is "fully on track to achieve our goals for gross margin, EBITDA margin and free cash flow margin."

Financial Results

  • Bandwidth reported total revenue of $754 million for the full year 2025, up 10% organically year-over-year, non-GAAP gross margin of 58%, adjusted EBITDA of $93 million, and free cash flow of $57 million.
  • Enterprise Voice customer category grew 21% for the year, driven by a record number of $1 million-plus deals. The enterprise cohort added in 2025 represents 15% of total enterprise revenue, with over 40% of growth from accounts added in the past three years.
  • Global Voice Plans revenue grew 8%, more than doubling compared to 2024. Programmable messaging grew 7% organically.
  • Software services revenue exited 2025 at a $15 million annualized run rate, ahead of previous expectations, and is now attached to all $1 million-plus deals.
  • Customer name retention rate remains above 99%, and organic net retention is 107%. Average annual revenue per customer ended 2025 at $232,000, up from $171,000 three years ago.

Q&A

  • Arjun Bhatia, William Blair & Company: Asked about Q4 trends in Enterprise Voice and the impact of large deals on 2026 growth. Raiford responded that annual growth was 21% and that "deployment and ramping into 2026" is expected to drive further growth. Software services are now critical to "nearly every deal."
  • Bhatia followed up on political campaign messaging, with Raiford stating "political campaign contribution this year will be roughly 2.5% of cloud communications revenue."
  • Erik Suppiger, B. Riley Securities: Questioned the difference between cloud communication growth (10%) and total revenue growth (16%). Raiford attributed the difference to "surcharge growth rate for carrier messaging surcharges," impacted by carrier price increases.
  • Suppiger also asked about competition from Twilio in voice, with Raiford responding "we're not" seeing more competition, noting recent customer wins were from other carriers.
  • Patrick Walravens, Citizens JMP Securities: Inquired about integration with Google Conversational AI and Bandwidth's competitive moat. Raiford explained Maestro's role in facilitating complex integrations and described the moat as "a mile wide, filled with oil and lit on fire," citing global infrastructure and structural margin advantages.
  • Joshua Reilly, Needham & Company: Asked about the pipeline for Voice AI, with Raiford stating "AI is a component of all enterprise conversations," and projecting 2026 as a "vital year for AI adoption to go from experimenting to real scale and deployment."
  • Reilly also asked about composition of $1 million-plus deals, with Chief Product Officer John Bell emphasizing the "move to AI is reinforcing the value proposition and showing up in these customer conversations."

Sentiment Analysis

  • Analysts maintained a positive tone, focused on enterprise growth, AI adoption, and revenue composition, with follow-up on political messaging and competitive dynamics.
  • Management's tone was confident during prepared remarks, frequently using phrases like "we are confident" and "on track to achieve our goals," and remained detailed and assertive in Q&A, especially when describing competitive moats and AI momentum.
  • Compared to the previous quarter, both analysts and management showed increased focus on AI voice adoption and large enterprise deals, with management expressing even greater confidence in financial and strategic targets.

Quarter-over-Quarter Comparison

  • The current quarter saw a shift from highlighting product launches and customer events to emphasizing record enterprise deal wins, AI developer engagement, and surpassing free cash flow goals.
  • Guidance for 2026 is more specific, with explicit projections for revenue, EBITDA margin, and EPS, while acknowledging headwinds in messaging revenue CAGR.
  • Analyst questions evolved from onboarding and product adoption timelines to deeper inquiries about AI monetization and competitive positioning.
  • Management's tone grew more assertive regarding Bandwidth's moat and durability, while analysts remained focused on the enterprise AI opportunity and revenue drivers.
  • Key metrics such as customer retention, software services revenue, and enterprise deal composition were given more prominence this quarter.

Risks and Concerns

  • Management noted that "market dynamics, particularly in messaging, will almost certainly keep us short of our multiyear revenue CAGR target."
  • Raiford highlighted the reliance on carrier surcharge pricing for total revenue growth differences.
  • Analysts raised questions about the impact of political campaign messaging and competitive threats from other CPaaS providers.
  • Management emphasized ongoing monitoring of political messaging contributions and continued investment in innovation to maintain competitive advantages.

Final Takeaway

Bandwidth enters 2026 with strong enterprise momentum, driven by accelerated adoption of AI-powered voice solutions, a record number of large enterprise deals, and robust financial performance. The company projects 16% revenue growth, a 20% EBITDA margin, and anticipates exceeding prior free cash flow targets, while launching its inaugural $80 million share repurchase program. Management underscores confidence in Bandwidth's business model, customer durability, and the expanding role of AI and software services as central to long-term value creation for shareholders.

Read the full Earnings Call Transcript

data.title: American States Water projects $185M–$225M in 2026 utility capex as rate base climbs 11.3% CAGR
data.description:
Earnings Call Insights: American States Water Company (AWR) Q4 2025

Management View

  • Robert Sprowls, CEO, President & Director, stated that "2025 was a very productive and positive year for the company." He highlighted that Golden State Water Company received the final decision from the CPUC on its general rate case for 2025 through 2027, and the electric subsidiary received its rate decision for 2023 through 2026. Both were described as "constructive regulatory outcomes and position us to continue investing in our utility infrastructure for safe and reliable services for generations to come."
  • The CEO announced, "we delivered strong financial results for 2025 with reported earnings that were $0.20 per share higher compared to 2024 or $0.33 per share higher as adjusted."
  • Sprowls detailed expansion, noting that Golden State Water completed a transaction to own and operate water and wastewater systems for a new community expected to reach 1,300 customer connections and received approval to provide water to another community projected to serve up to 3,800 connections in five years and 17,500 dwelling units long term. Additionally, Golden State Water signed an agreement to acquire water system assets in Norwalk, serving about 900 customers.
  • The company invested $210.9 million in regulated utility infrastructure in 2025 and raised the quarterly dividend by 8.3% for its 71st consecutive year of increases.
  • Eva Tang, Senior VP of Finance, CFO, Corporate Secretary & Treasurer, reported, "Adjusted earnings for AWR consolidated increased $0.18 per share over the prior year when excluding the impact of a onetime tax benefit recorded in the fourth quarter of 2024." Tang added, "reported consolidated earnings were $0.74 per share as compared to $0.75 per share for the fourth quarter of 2024 or $0.56 per share as adjusted for that quarter."

Outlook

  • The company projects 2026 company-funded capital expenditures to reach $185 million to $225 million.
  • Robert Sprowls indicated ASUS earnings are projected at $0.63 to $0.67 per share for 2026.
  • Sprowls reiterated confidence in sustained rate base growth, stating, "Golden State Water anticipates a robust and sustained growth in its rate base over the next few years."

Financial Results

  • Consolidated earnings for the full year 2025 were $3.37 per share as reported versus $3.17 per share for 2024. Adjusted, 2025 earnings were $3.37 per share compared to $3.04 per share for 2024, representing a $0.33 per share increase.
  • Golden State Water reported $0.50 per share for the quarter, compared to $0.52 per share in 2024, or $0.39 per share as adjusted.
  • Electric segment reported $0.11 per share for the quarter, compared to $0.13 per share in 2024, or a $0.04 per share increase as adjusted.
  • ASUS earnings were $0.16 per share for the quarter, up from $0.11 per share last year.
  • Consolidated revenue for the quarter increased by $21.2 million compared to 2024, with water segment revenue growing by $17.4 million and ASUS revenue by $9.5 million.
  • Net cash provided by operating activity was $229.7 million for 2025, up from $198.7 million in 2024.
  • The company raised $67 million under its at-the-market offering program during the year.

Q&A

  • There were no analyst questions during this quarter’s call, as confirmed by the operator: "At this time, there is no one in our queue. So this concludes our question-and-answer session."

Sentiment Analysis

  • With no analyst participation, there was no expression of external sentiment. Management maintained a positive and confident tone during prepared remarks, emphasizing regulatory achievements, rate base growth, dividend increases, and strong financial results. Sprowls described the year as "very productive and positive" and reiterated confidence in future growth.
  • Compared to the previous quarter, where analysts raised detailed questions on ASUS contracts, rate base translation, and M&A, this quarter’s absence of analyst interaction eliminated any direct challenges or skepticism, resulting in a uniformly positive management narrative.

Quarter-over-Quarter Comparison

  • The company continued to report robust earnings growth and capital investment, with adjusted EPS rising by $0.33 per share year-over-year.
  • Guidance for ASUS earnings in 2026 was maintained at $0.63 to $0.67 per share, consistent with prior communication.
  • Rate base growth was highlighted, with the adopted average water rate base increasing from $980.4 million in 2021 to $1.673.2 billion in 2026, a 11.3% compound annual growth rate.
  • The absence of analyst Q&A this quarter contrasted with the previous quarter’s active discussion on contract timing, rate base calculation, and strategic direction, providing fewer external challenges and a more unopposed management perspective.
  • Management’s tone remained confident and forward-looking in both quarters, but the lack of questioning in Q4 removed the critical dialogue seen previously.

Risks and Concerns

  • Sprowls cautioned that "without the continuation of a full revenue decoupling mechanism and a full cost balancing account for water supply, the company may be subject to future volatility in revenues and earnings as a result of fluctuations in water consumption by its customers and changes in water supply source mix."
  • The company noted a nearly 4% decrease in water consumption compared to amounts adopted in the general rate case but stated that the net impact from source mix and consumption fluctuations "did not have a material impact on Golden State Water's 2025 earnings."
  • Management acknowledged exposure to volatility due to regulatory changes and customer usage patterns but highlighted ongoing infrastructure investment and expansion efforts.

Final Takeaway

American States Water Company reported a strong finish to 2025, driven by favorable regulatory outcomes, robust rate base growth, and continued expansion in both regulated and contracted service businesses. The company invested heavily in infrastructure, executed strategic acquisitions, and delivered higher adjusted earnings per share, while projecting continued capital investment and earnings growth in 2026. Management emphasized confidence in the company’s outlook, highlighting both its dividend track record and its ability to navigate regulatory and operational challenges as it scales its customer and asset base.

Read the full Earnings Call Transcript

data.title: RingCentral GAAP EPS of $0.26 misses by $0.01, revenue of $644M in-line
data.description:

    • GAAP EPS of $0.26 compared to ($0.08) last year.
    • Net cash provided by operating activities of $149 million, up 12.0% year over year.

data.title: Harmonic Non-GAAP EPS of $0.06 misses by $0.04, revenue of $157.3M beats by $16.32M
data.description:
• Harmonic press release (HLIT): Q4 Non-GAAP EPS of $0.06 misses by $0.04.
  • Revenue of $157.3M (-29.2% Y/Y) beats by $16.32M.
